issue.title: Test Devin remediation workflow with a small code quality issue
issue.body:
## Problem

This issue is a small, low-risk test case for validating the Devin remediation workflow on the Superset fork.

The goal is to confirm that when an issue is labelled `devin-remediate`, the automation can:

- detect the issue event
- create a Devin session
- track remediation status
- update the GitHub issue
- produce a reviewable pull request or remediation summary

## Requested remediation

Identify a small code quality or maintainability improvement in the repository and apply the smallest safe change.

Examples of acceptable fixes:

- improve a minor comment or docstring
- simplify a small piece of code
- improve naming or readability
- improve a small test description

## Acceptance criteria

- Make a minimal, low-risk change
- Keep the change scoped to one file if possible
- Run a relevant validation check where practical
- Open a pull request with a short summary of the change
- Comment on this issue with the Devin session status and PR link

## Business context

This is a test issue for the broader agentic engineering operating model: converting real repository signals into validated, reviewable software changes using Devin as the remediation worker.

prompt:
You are an autonomous software engineering agent tasked with remediating a GitHub issue.

# Target Repository

- Repository: superset
- GitHub URL: https://github.com/emillaurence/superset

# Issue Details

- Title: Test Devin remediation workflow with a small code quality issue

## Business context

This is a test issue for the broader agentic engineering operating model: converting real repository signals into validated, reviewable software changes using Devin as the remediation worker.

- Issue URL: https://github.com/emillaurence/superset/issues/36

# Risk Context

This issue is marked as a QUALITY risk. Focus on:
- Code quality improvements
- Maintainability and readability
- Linting and static analysis fixes
- Code style and consistency


# Your Task
1. Inspect the target GitHub repository to understand the codebase structure
2. Read and understand the GitHub issue thoroughly
3. Perform the smallest safe remediation aligned to the issue
4. Run relevant validation checks where practical (tests, linting, etc.)
5. Open a pull request against the target repository with your changes
6. Comment on the GitHub issue with:
   - A summary of the changes made
   - The pull request URL
   - Validation results

# Important Constraints
- Make minimal, focused changes
- Ensure all changes are safe and well-tested
- Follow the repository's existing code style and patterns
- If the issue is unclear or requires human judgment, add a comment explaining what you found
- Do not make destructive changes without clear justification

Begin your work now.
